CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Sunday’s gathering was a great demonstration.
Vavious people may say this or that of it, they may
criticise the speeches, but one fact that stands out
beyond legitimate challenge is that it was a great, or-
derly, and colossal demonstration. Bub even as a
demonstration the libeller of Canon McInerney made
a ridiculous effort to minimise it. Iiven ‘' the ’I’Jnghc
sunshine ”’ is laid hold of by the * Irish Times ™ as a
peg, if we may put it that way, on WhlC]}‘tO I.\‘ung_ a
considerable portion of the meeting. The ** Irish
Times "’ remarked :—" X

““Those whom the bright sunshine would have
brought out of doors in any event, bub who would
probably have turned countrywards in the ordinary

course, yesterday concentrated in the centre of
the city to see what was afoot. The youths and
girls who always frequent Sackville Streef'on a
Sunday, numbers of labouring men welcoming an
occupation for their idle day, people from the
suburbs come in to see what was happening and
to hear the bands, and the large contingent of
loungers always available to swell a crowd in
Dublin—these formed a large proportion of the
gathering in Sackville Street.’”

The fact that the * Irish Times '’ was driven to that
sort of attempt to belittle the demonstration is elo-,
quent evidence of its imposing dimensions. People go
to such demonstrations to demonstrate and not neces-
sarily to hear speeches. It was impossible for large
numbers on the outer fringes of the several crowds to
hear the speeches, and so they demonstrated by stand-
ing and chatting amongst the crowds. We ‘did not
hear one word of the speeches. It was practically
impossible to get near enough to No. 2 platform from
the Pillar, and in order to get to No. 1 platform we had
to go down Abbey Street, turn up Marlborough Street,
wallk up North Great George’s Street, and come down .
Rutland Square, to find that we could get no further
than Cavendish Row. Certainly as a demonstration
it was a great turn cut. In one part of its report the
anti-Trish paper says that ‘‘ the number of them round
the platforms was not all that was expected,”’ whilst
in its report of the proceedings at the Redmond plat-
form, it says, ‘‘ but the voice of the speaker could not
possibly have reached half of those about it.”” So ac-
cording to ‘“ Alf Iox "’ half of those round No. 1 plat-
form could not hear Redmond, and yet it was not
satisfied.

The “* Trish Times *’ gives three-quarters of a column
to the doings of the imitation Suffragettes in connec-
tion with the demonstration. The *‘ Irish Times’
account is most sympathetic. Anything that might be
used against the Home Rule cause is eagerly grabbed
at by Arnott’s paper. By the way, did anyone miss
the vast battalions of the ‘“ Hungavians ?”’

It appears that Skeffy was amongst the girls fli
leaflets at the crowd. Kettle appears to have left the
girls to their fate at this juncture. Kettle; by the way,
did not say anything about that £62 10s., to which we
referred last week, and Oldham, the man who is out
for freedom and not for money, did not speak at all.

nging

It is just as well to keep before the public the dirty
work that is being done in England by the Unionist
Association of Ulster. We put it down here as stated
in part of Mr. Dillon’s speech on Sunday:—

Now, what are the methods at present adopted
by the agents of the Unionists of Ulster to run_
down Home Rule and to malign and slander their
own countrymen? You have some hundreds of
men in the pay of the Unionist Association of
Ulster, earning what I must describe as a dis-
graceful living, a shameful living, by going about
from house to house, in England, slandering and
blackguarding and fouling the country that gave
them birth. I don’t think there is & more ignoble,
or a more shameful, calling, and T don’t envy the
man who talkes to that method of making a living.

‘
We have a great evil in this country in the shape of
- the importation of ‘ penny dirties ’; but this country

'iu.ibga turn is an exporter of what we might call *“ thirty
shilling dirties.”

. The Bungs in the vicinity of 0'Connell Strect mado
well out of the demonstration. Everything considered

the number we noticed the worse of Bung were very °

few indeed, and when we consider what the ** thirty
shilling dirties "’ are doing in England, we are not at
all certain that their paymasters would not attempt to
organise a few tipsy fellows with a view to attempting
to throw discredit on the demonstration. And, by the
way, talking of Bungs and the demonstration, we hear
that & Bung T.C., one Alf. Byrne, of Talbot Street,
hung.out a flag from his bungery bearing the inscrip-
tion “‘ North Dock Ward U.I.L.” An explanation of
that is called for. It is worth recording that M.
Drury, Divisional Magistrate, in the Southern Police
Court, said that he did not think there was any city in
the United Kingdom where such a large demonstration
would have passed off in the same orderly and credit-
able manner.

Collecting money for the boy of the King-Harman’s
is not such easy work. Up to the time of the last
meeting only £25 12s. 6d. had been subscribed, and
the illuminated address is to cost about £14. A dis-
play of fireworks was suggested, but one man said
they would have no money in hands, and another very
truly remarked, * too much fireworks -we have.”

There is to be a fair in Portarlington on Good Friday
and some of the Portarlington Bungs applied to the
magistrates for permission to open during prohibited
hours! What will Bung ask next?  Happily the
magistrates refused the request.

In view of the drilling that is alleged to be going on
amongst the Orangemen, it would be no harm to hurry
up the organisation of Nationalist boys’ brigades. The
Hibernian Boys’ Brigade should have a big future
before it. We understand they are shortly to be estal-
lished in Drogheda. As the Orangemen are appar-
ently allowed to drill, it is just as well that young Ire-
land' should pour into the ranks: of the boys’ brigades
and learn—shall we say—deportment, and the Irish
language.

In connection with Uisneach Teis in Athlone, an
industrial exhibition will be held early in June. One
of the features of the exhibition will be a cottage
scheme. This will be divided into two sections, the
cottage section and the cottage and garden section, both
confined to the Athlone Urban area. Applications for
plans of the hall where the industrial exhibition will Le
held should be made to either of the Hon. Secs, Mus.
P. C. V. Murtagh, Avalreagh, . Athlone, or Mr. J. J.
Kelly, Assistant Manager, Athlone Woollen Mills.

A NOTICE TO QUIT.

Oh, Tim, within my heavy heart
I hear the ringing cheers,
That from the myriad people start
When Redmond’s crowd appears.
From Parnell’s statue down to Dan’s
I hear each surging roar,
Which tells that William and his clans
Are doomed for evermore.

I hear a hundred clashing bands.
How they do tantalise |

I see the flags and gilded stands
And all my heart do prize.

Bub now that pomp and glitter sweet,
So dear to me of yore,

Spells dark disaster and retreat
TFor William evermore.

The man is bound to get a drop
And find his level sure,

Who aims to be a nation's prop,
The only Simon Pure.

He must as great dictator pass,
Until the people sore,

Tell Cincinnatus he’s an ass,
Not wanted evermore.

This Dublin meeting rings the knell
Of Faction’s parting day,

And now, methinks, I may as well
Prepare to clear away.

The day of sham retiring’s past
And limelight scenes are o’er;

The next go off will be the last
TFor William evermore.

But, oh! ’tis hard to have to go
Without a'flash and crack,
A big sensation that would throw
A glare upon my track.
Sighs, cheers and bombshells, grief and rage
Are wings on which I'd soar
With deep content from Ireland stage
To silence evermore.

. Aprir, 6,:1912.

To sweet conciliation's friends
I now may bid adieu.
Ye Irish landlords, William sends
His fondest love to you.
To all who kindled faction’s fire,
To dearest Barrymore,
My best regards ere I retire
An exile evermore. A. M. W.
Lingland has her future behind Ler. Though there
must be considerable reserve force left in that country
yeb, it seems to us that she has to a great extent run
herself out, and that her problems—the problems that
are the resultant of corruption and prosperity-—place
her face to face before a very ominous futu The
*‘glorious Reformation’” has been steadily working her
to the brink of the precipice. She b cen living on her
Christian—-that is, her old Catholic—capital; and as
she retained a good many points of Christianity and
Christian tradition, the capital has lasted her a very
ong time. It is becoming nearly exhausted now. The
Linglish have only very confused and varying moral
standards. In England: id in Bnglish  Ireland—
iberty is confused with licence.

is more, some of the latter putting on if industry
and cornmerce were low things. The overpaid Govern-

ment officials dawdle along, and even some feather-
headed tradespeople try to imitate them. Riches in
the ages of strong faith must of necessity have been
tempered with justice, mercy, and good will to all:
riches in an age of no faith or of half faith—and the
Anglicised Irish cannot be charged with much more
than half faifh—must lead to trouble and revolution.
For the rich get more and more selfish and arrogant,
and the poor more and more selfish and resentful—
and: there you have the stage clear for a fight in which
modern civilization may go down. The sooner we geb
Home Rule the better, for in a state of Home Rule
there is a probability that Ireland will look life straight
in the face and make a clean sweep of bridge players,
golf idlers, ‘“ At Home ” fools, and shallow people
generally, including ladies who carry pug dogs in trams
and “‘ gents ” who wear canary gloves and bracelet
waflches.

THE:LEADER.

The Suffragettes ave placed on the shelf for the pre-
sent. The antics: of the  Irish: imitation Suffragettes
who went to disturb ‘the Churchill meeting at Belfast
rightly disgusted many Nationalists. The letter: sent
to the members of the Irish Party by the secretary of
the Irish Women’s Suffrage Federation was most im-
pudent. These fanatics want to hen-peck the coun-
try: they would be wise to limit- themselves to. hen-
pecking their poor husbands, in those cases where they
have such. In Ireland tle imitation Suffragettes are
partly associated with the non-mere Irish, and in some
other cases they are known cranks. Mrs. Cousins,
secretary of one of the Suffragette Leagues, wrote a
letter to some Sufiragette organisation in England.
The letter was one of those seized by the:Iinglish
police ab the-offices of the Women’s Social and Poli-
tical Union in England. In the course of this letter
Mus. Cousins explained the sending of only six Suffra-
gettes from Ireland to the recent London demonstra-
tion on the ground that *‘ a great deal of local militant
work will have to be done on the occasion of the
National Convention.”” That lets the cat out of the
bag. Some of the militant Suffragettes in Ireland are
probably Nationalists, but a good many of them are,
at least, sour non-Nationalists, to put it at the mildest.
A movement that could be used to create annoyance

at a National Convention, as at the Churchill meeting *

at Belfast, would naturally be used for all it was worth
by the anti-Home Rulers and Carrion Crows. We
confess that politically we do not like the imitation
Suffragette gang in Ireland, and since they did their
little worst in Belfast, and contemplate giving trouble
at the National Convention, we like them less. The
assurance with which the coterie of Sufiragettes in
Treland assume to speak on behalf of Irishwomen shows
how utterly bereft they are of a sense of humour.
Tvishwomen are laughing at them. The Hon. Sec. of
** The Trishwomen’s Suffrage Federation’’ commenced
her letter to the members of the Irish Parliamentary
Party this way:— If your Party refuses to support
our Conciliation Bill on the 28th, we shall take it as an
act of hostility to Irishwomen.”” The next will be
Skefty getting up to speak on behalf of the women of
the Trish race ab home and abroad! If the coterie f
Suffragettes in Ireland think that they can bully the
country they are demonstrating their lack of judgment.
We were glad that the Suffragette Bill was defeated:
and we believe all Trishwomen outside the coterie were
delighted also.

What are the Vigilance Committees doing?  When
it comes to tackling matters at close quarters we can
quite understand that some of the members are shy:
they lile to be great local Catholics and pillars of public
morality so long as it costs nothing beyond passing a
unanimous resolution. When it comes to visiting
Waterford newsagents we understand that several of
the members of the Waterford Vigilance Committee
are shy and full of excuses. But we believe there are
sufficient members on the Waterford Committee cap-
able of cairying on the campaign with reasonable
vigour. Why are not the meetings called more regu-
larly ? In Waterford there has not been a meeting for
upwards of a month, and the officials appear to have
gone asleep. We hear that some of the committee
think there are only seven or eight dirty papers, and
that having black-listed these, and having called on
the newsagents not to sell them, they think apparently
they can rest. There are various papers that so far
as we know are not black-listed that are ‘‘ dirties.”
Papers, however comparatively clean they may be
otherwise, that have dirty advertisements, are certainly
dirty papers, and should be campaigned against.
‘hear that the Kilkenny Vigilance Committee is ir
again : however, we would like to hear what it is stir-
ring. Is there any Vigilance Committee in Killarney ?

We showed last week, we think conclusively, that
Professor Oldham could not reasonably give less than
£300 to the Home Rule Fund. And now he sends us
two letters, one addressed to Mr. Redmond and an-
other to Mr. Dalton, which indicate that he is cross
with the Party, and probably will excuse himself from
subscribing at alll  Isn’t it very slim of the wily Pro-
fessor!  And he has out-slimmed Professor Kettle,
whom we showed should give at least £62 10s. to the
Home Rule Fund. Oldham is hiding behind the petti-
coats of the Suffers, but Kettle has apparently reneged
the sereeching sisterhood : Oldham has made himself
ridiculous, but being a tough old stager e may console
himself by saying that even if he is ridiculous he has
kept his three hundred pounds. The Irish Party
adopted a certain attitude on a recent Bill about votes
for women, and Oldham put on his war paint. Kettle
appears, in comparison with his fellow Professor, to
have taken the blow lying down. As results—Kettle
talked raimezs on Sunday, but Oldham on principle
refused to wag his silver tongue. The Professor whose

MIDLAND GREAT WESTERN RAILWAY
OF IRELAND COMPANY.

TXAMINATION FOR CLERKSHIPS.

Notice is hereby given that a Competitive
Examination for Clerkships in the service of the
above Company will be held on Thursday, 23rd
May, 1912.

Candidates, who on that date must be under 17 and
not over 20 of age, will be examined in (a) the
following three subjects:—

1. English Composition, including handwriting and
spelling.

2. Arithmetic, up to and including Vulgar and
Decimal Fractions, with special attention to Practice.
3. Geography, with particular reference to the Com-
mercial Geography of Ireland and Great Britain ; know-
ledge of the lines of communication in and between
the Three Kingdoms will be expected.

And (b) at their option, in either or both of the two
further subjects:—

4. Pitman’s Shorthand.

5. Trish.

Candidates will be required to appear before a
Committee of the Board for approval as to character
ugd suitability at 2.0 p.m. on Monday, 13th May,
1912.

Successful Candidates will have to pass the Com-=
pany’s doctor.

Application of Candidate, together with Registrar’s
Certificate of Birth, two testimonials, and an
entrance fee of 10s., must be sent to the Secretary
not later than 10th day of May, 1912, and the
Candidate must then specify which optional subject
or subjects (if any) he desires to .present for
Examination.

The commencing salary of Clerks will be 15s. per
week, and will increase according to revised scale to
30s. per week, subsequent increases to be by merit
and position.  Clerks will be taken on as Probationers
for nine months, and not appointed to the Company’s
service until the expiration of that time, and then only
provided they have been satisfactorily reported upon
by their superior officer.

PERCY A. HAY, Secretary.

Broadstone Terminus, Dublin,

26th March, 1912.

{1 HERE seems to be a lull in the campaign against

the sale of the * penny dirties.”” At all events
it looks as if some of the Vigilance Committees through
the country are beginning to relax their vigilance while
the enemy is yet lurking in our midst. There is now
more need than ever to keep a watchful eye on the
erstwhile vendors of the ‘* dirties.”” There are many
journals with nice names that are reeking with moral
poison; the objectionable portion of the contents is
generally found in the advertisement pages. The mode
of procedure adopted by our Vigilance Committees would
require, I think, some alteration if the literature
““microbe *’ is to be wiped out. During the last few
months the Vigilance Committees have black-listed a
certain number of cross-Channel papers that were
known to be highly pernicious and immoral long before
the present campaign was inaugurated. It was neces-
sary, of course, to have something to go on, in order
to bind the newsvendors to a pledge that they would
not sell objectionable papers. But for some reason or
another many bad papers were not included in the list,

A MEMORY OF THE WEST.

‘N] I were assembled, teachers from the four winds

o) of Birinn, for the annual Taster Congress in the
Citie of the Tribes. Toregather g—to utilize some
spare howrs previous to the weighty discussions, a
-party of us chartered a waggonette and a pair of stout:
ponies, and proceeded to explore the rocky coast road
which leads to Barna and Spiddal. The afternoon was
one of delicious spring freshness, and the slight April
showers which came and went with surprising sudden-
ness only intensified our eagerness to be out and about.
As we approached the first-named village we were
struck with the amazing quantities of stones which were
here and there and everywhere—now heaped in huge
masses by the roadside, now forming the ditches
which bounded tiny fields not larger than a good sized
quilt. ~ To our stranger eyes, it looked as if stones had
been rained on this bare shoreland from the deluge,
and I am sure elicited the emphatic remark of one of
our Northern colleagues, when, after silently watching
the stcoped forms of a man and woman manuring the
patches of soil with seaweed, he cried: ““ And you will
hear th he Irish people are lazy! Why, instead of
paying rent, they should be paid to live here.”” De-
pressed insensibly with the dreary human outlook, we
resolutely turned our eyes to where tender signs of
spring were making themselves known.  Yes! on the
mountain’sides were bubbling down:

‘“ The streams released, that surging flow,
From craggy shelves, down sylvan glades.””
And, again, gladsome sight to city-bred eyes, the in-
o tuf's of primreses—

““ Which star the rock, and o’er
The woodpath shed their milky way.””

When, suddenly, adown the winding road, came a fa.info,
moaning, mysterious note.  So heart-thrilling was it
that, as we mutually gazed-at cach other, our blanched
faces showed our horror, which decpened as the
sound was repeated. No one spoke, however, and our
relief was great when we rounded a turn on the roa.d’-
and came on a peasant funeral, with two 1<gene,3¥s
heading it.  Yes! this, then, was the *‘caoine’ or
ancient, Celtic death wail! At intervals was it uttered,
and surely its intense, heartfelt sorrow will never leave
the memories of those who heard it. The train was a
long one.  Tollowing the coffin, which was borne on a
country carb, were the women mourners, tall, vigorous,
and splendid specimens of country womanhood, garbed
in their bright-hued petticoats of glowing red or blue,
little shawls enveloped their shoulders, and the sole

WANTED—A NATIONAL LITERATURE.

ITHIN the last few months there have been
many discussions on the question cf the litera-
ture that is invading this country. Week by week
newspaper shops are flooded with all sorts and kinds of
publications that come from the cheap Press of Great
Britain. The Fnglish are a wise people, they know how
to make their profits, and they have succeeded in
establishing the means of an extraordinary sale of their
mental wares in this Island. The way for them to do
so was certainly open. It is easier to pour in some-
thing where there is nothing than to replace with one
thing another that must be got rid of first. ,There has
been practically no Irish Literature—for what exists
hardly counts in the face of the incoming tide. ~ The
causes of this state of things are manifold, but the
major cause is that Ireland has broken with her past,
and has set up an alien culture in the place of her own.
Her writers, possessed of no literary ancestry, did not
feel the claim of a bygone heritage, and gave, without
stint or hesitation, their powers to the Press of another
counfry. Thus the most gifted ceased to labour in
the field of national culture, and that field, become
rank and barren, served as the dust-bin for the garbage
flung info it from the other side of the Channel. Day .
by day the evil grew, and the Irish youth, whose minds
were thus distorted, seized more and more eagerly on
this gutter Press. =~ We have but to turn back the
pages of our Liistory, we have but to enter into conversa-
tion with some veteran of the West to realize something
of the old native culture. ~Where the English literary
refuse has not come to destroy and where the traditions
still remain, there is in the most unlettered: person a
distinctive intellectual quality.  Side by side, there-
fore, they can be placed: the Irishman of the older
generation, possessing his remnant of Gaelic culture
and who, perhaps, has never opened any book, and the
young man whose literary education has been formed
on the Sunday paper and the sixpenny novelette. The
contrast is saddening in the extreme, for it means that
an entire mental revolution is taking place in Ireland,
and that if it continues unchecked all that we most
prize of our heritage must be lost. ‘‘The land is a
great thing,’’ wrote the late Mr. William Bulfin, *“ but
it is not everything. ~Mind and character are greater
things. Manhood and a sense of citizenship are greater
things.  Knowledge and culture are greater things.
Let us fight for them too.””

Ireland intellectually is in a strange position.  She
is a country apart; her children are of a distinctive race
of which they profess to be proud. Yet she is satisfied
to accept the erumbs of mental food dropped from her

The Press is not sufficient for the building up of a
national literature, though it goes far towards it. Books
play a !s.rge part therein. Many by Ivish writers and
expressing Irish sentiments are published every year,
but comparatively few are brought out in this country.
Those written in Irish are, of course, published over
here, and they exceed in number the Iinglish-written
books printed in Treland. Still one would like to seeit pos-
sible for Irish writers of all kinds to publish at home. ~ If
it were made possible for them to do so, it would benefit
this country not only materially by giving more em-
ployment, but also intellectually as Irish publishers
could encourage more national sentiment in Irish
authors. Several Carnegie Libraries have been founded
in Ireland. What books are therein contained? Good
books surely, but is not their tendency to Anglicize?
Anglicising is not bad in itself, and in England it is the
development of the English people on their own best
lines. ~ But in Ireland its effect is the reverse. It
takes us off our own lines and cuts us adrift from all
that is most inspiriting and ennobling in the Gaelic
race, and makes of us but indifferent imitators. Libra-
ries that result in this pave the way for the mental
destruction of the Inish race. It may seem narrow-
minded to look at things from a national point of view.
But it is not more narrow than the Anglo standpoint.
Every point of view may in some sense be said to be
narrow. The question is to choose the most natural,
the most suitable, in fact, the best for the individual.
It is obvious then which should be the standpoint of
Irishmen and women. What really matters are the
elemental things of life. We have them here in Ireland
as they are elsewhere. =~ We can work them up and
clothe them with the traditions and culture of our race.
This system is the foundation of every national litera-
ture.

Is it not something to be glad of that at this day of the
world’s history when so much has been already redone,
resaid and rewritten, that here in Treland we have still
an unexplored territory, a mental field that has not yet
bloomed to full life, and that but awaits the intelli-
gence, the labours, and the sacrifice, without which
nothing can be done, to burst into harvest.

CORRESPONDENCE.

ST. PATRICK’S DAY IN ROME.
A CORRECTION OF A MISREPRESENTATION.

Dublin, 1st April, 1912.

DEeAR SIR,—In the issue of the “ Catholic Times,”’ of
22nd March, the Very Rev. Dr. O’Keane, O.P., preach-
ing in the Church of St. Isidore in Rome, is represented
as making the following observations in the course of
his Panegyric on St. Patrick :—

““ Son of Irish soil, I hang my head for shame when
T recall that to compel the metropolis of Ireland to
keep the 17th March as a holiday, therc was needed a
statute of the Protestant Parliament of England.”
Would you permit me to enter a strong protest against
the assertion made by the reverend preacher. I
do so with greab reluctance and after much hesitation:
for it might; be open to misconstruction and be taken
as an attack on the independence of a priest preaching,

THE LEADER.

Aprir, 6, 1912.

which js a thing T abhor. Dr. O'Keane, however, ex—
poses Irish Cz\ﬁlolics to the danger of gross misrepre-
sentation in the very centre of Christendom.  He
asserts that it required a statute passcd by a Protestant
Parliament to compel the Catholics of Dublin to keep
St. Patrick’s Day as a holiday. It is a half truth and
must be forgiven as an oratorical embellishment. It is
true it requived a statute of a Protestant Parliament to
make St. Patrick’s Day a legal holiday, but it is not the
whole truth, inasmuch as the statute was passed at
the instance of the Irish Catholic and Protestant Mem-
bers of Parliament. Dr. O’Keane must know that i
Irish official life, as represented at the Vicercgal‘ Lodge,
Catholic traditions and customs are treated with con-
tempt. The Levees, Drawingrooms, ete., are all
held in Lent, but the Irish people practically
take no part in them. TFurther, the Corpora-
tion of Dublin (a body under the control of
the people for thirty years before the passing
of the statute referred to) had proclaimed the 17th of
March a Municipal Holiday, and it was not the will of
the people of Dublin that St. Patrick’s Day was not a
legal holiday. Having regard to the personal sacrifices
made both by priests and people, prior to the passing
of the Act referred to by Dr. O'Keane, to fulfil the
obligation to hear Mass on our Patron’s Day, it is hardly
fair for him to hold Ireland’s metropolis up to scorn in
Rome. If Dublin workmen have risen at 4.30 a.m.,
and if shop girls, dressmalkers, clerks, ete., ete., have
all risen an hour earlier than usual, in order to get to
Mass on the 17th of March prior to going to work, I do
not think there is much to hang the head in shame
about. March winds in Ireland are not cxactly zephyrs,
and it requires some courage for workpeople to face the
cold an hour earlier than usual, and 1 dare assert that
there are more working people going to Mass in Dublin
daily, much less on holidays, than in any other city of
its size on the Continent of Kurope.

Our 1,500 years unbroken fidelity to our Faith might
have spared us this misrepresentation of the rev.
preacher. It is not desirable it should go unchal-
lenged, although it may have been made te adorn a
tale or point a moral. ~ It was uttered almost on the
steps of the Papal Throne, and is liable to create a false
impression. The enemies of Treland are always active
in Rome, and, as a layman, I cnter my protest against
the statement, and trust Dr. O’Keane will take an
early opportunity of correcting the false impression
amongst Italians, French and others, which I know he
has created.

Yours faithfully,
P. J. DANIEL.
